comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
I wrote a complete proof to solution.tex.

**Result:** The problem is in $\mathsf{P}$ (in fact linear time, and even $\mathsf{AC}^0$/logspace). It is trivial, governed by the criterion:

> $L^*$ is infinite $\iff$ $L$ contains at least one word of length $\ge 1$.

**Proof of the criterion (two lemmas):**

1. *Sufficiency:* If $w\in L$ with $|w|=\ell\ge 1$, then $w^k\in L^*$ for all $k\ge 0$, and $|w^k|=k\ell$ is strictly increasing in $k$, so $\{w^k\}$ are pairwise distinct and $L^*$ is infinite.

2. *Necessity (contrapositive):* If $L$ has no nonempty word, then $L\subseteq\{\varepsilon\}$. Any concatenation of copies of $\varepsilon$ is $\varepsilon$ (including the empty concatenation $k=0$), so $L^*=\{\varepsilon\}$, which is finite.

**Algorithm:** scan the input once, accept iff some listed word has $\ge 1$ symbol; $O(m)$ time. Hence in $\mathsf{P}$.

**On NP-hardness:** Since the problem is in $\mathsf{P}$, it is not $\mathsf{NP}$-hard unless $\mathsf{P}=\mathsf{NP}$ (composing such a reduction with the poly-time decider would put NP in P). So under $\mathsf{P}\neq\mathsf{NP}$ it is not NP-hard.

I verified the characterization numerically (enumerating $L^*$ up to bounded length for several test languages including $\emptyset$, $\{\varepsilon\}$, $\{a\}$, $\{\varepsilon,a\}$, $\{ab,ba\}$): finiteness occurs exactly when no nonempty word is present. LaTeX toolchain not installed in workspace, so compilation was skipped, but the file is self-contained amsart with standard packages.

Issue resolved.
